dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT. What brought you to call 911 today?
I've been short of breath for two days. The albuterol helps briefly, but it comes back.
On a scale from alert to unresponsive, how are you feeling right now?
I'm alert and aware. I can see you clearly.
Can you tell me your name and the date?
My name is Jane. I'm 66 years old.
Your airway is self-maintained, and your breathing sounds a bit wheezy. Do you have any difficulty clearing your throat?
We've already noted some expiratory wheezing and rales bilaterally in your lungs.
I see nasal flaring and rapid breathing. Let's measure your rate and depth.
Your respiratory rate was 36 at first. It's improved to 30 now.
Your lips and nail beds look a bit cyanotic. We'll start you on O2 immediately.
You're breathing with some effort-shoulder lift and chest expansion seems uneven.
Let's check for signs of poor perfusion. Where in your body are you feeling the most cold or numb?
Pulse is 104 and regular. We've noted tachycardia on the cardiac monitor.
Your skin is cool, cyanotic, and diaphoretic. Do you feel clammy?
Yes, I feel a bit sweaty and shaky.
I noticed your skin lacks moisture-abnormal turgor. Any rash or bruising?
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive. Trachea is midline and no facial deformity noted.
Any nasal obstruction, drainage, or hearing loss today?
No, my breathing is just wheezy and uncomfortable.
Heart sounds are regular. We see sinus tachycardia on the cardiac monitor and 4-lead ECG.
We placed you on a cardiac monitor with 4-lead monitoring at 7:48 to assess rhythm continuously.
A 12-lead EKG was obtained at 7:53-still showing sinus tachycardia but no acute ST changes.
We noted jugular venous distension. Are you having any difficulty swallowing?
No, but my head has ached for four days now.
Neck is flexible, no pain on flexion.
Abdomen is soft and nontender. No masses or obvious cause of pain.
Your legs are intact with a stable pelvis, but we see surgical staples from a left femoral repair.
Motor function is intact in all four extremities (+PMSx4).
All right, let's take a few minutes to talk through your health history.
You mentioned no history of respiratory conditions. Were you previously on a blood thinner?
No, I was never prescribed blood thinners.
We read the PCR note about a femur fracture and a previous hospitalization at [COMPANY_NAME]. You were intubated after flatlining?
Yes. I was told I flatlined. I woke up in the ICU, tube in my throat.
We see allergies to Penicillin, Ampicillin, and sulfonamides. Any new reactions recently?
I'm on Fludrocortisone, Metoprolol, and some others. No changes.
You also have known issues with GERD and hyperlipidemia from the chart. Is that still true?
Yes, I still struggle with reflux but never had heartburn for the past two days.
Describe exactly what led up to the 911 call?
They gave me nebulizers, and it helped for a bit, but then I started to breathe worse last night.
You mentioned mild pain in your left thigh. When did that start?
Does the leg pain get worse when you move it?
How would you describe the pain? Sharp, dull, throbbing?
It's a constant dull ache. Already had surgery on it.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst you've ever felt, how bad is the leg pain?
About a 2.
When did this most recent pain start yesterday?
Around bedtime. I just chalked it up to post-op stiffness.
I'll check your pulse. The initial reading was 104, but it's still at 104.
Your breathing is back to 28 per minute-still tachypneic, but improving.
First BP was 150 over 78. I just got 127 over 85-stable yet slightly lower.
Blood sugar dropped from 153 initially to zero during transport for some reason.
Your oxygen saturation improved from 91% up to 100% with nebulizer treatment.
Possible left atrial enlargement and short QT noted on 12-lead, but no acute infarction signs.
We successfully placed an IV cannula in your left arm at 7:55 using a 20 gauge needle.
At 7:48, we started oxygen via nebulizer with Albuterol and Atrovent-both improved your breathing slightly.
At 7:57, we gave you 10 mg Dexamethasone to reduce inflammation-your breathing seemed to stabilize after that.
Based on EKG and vitals, we followed protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress.
We still consider pulmonary embolism as a possible cause due to your immobility and prior hospitalization.
Asthma exacerbation remains a key consideration, especially with prior albuterol response and wheezing.
We'll take a final round of vitals now. Oxygen is still at 99% and BP holds at 127 over 85.
Your IV is secure, and the nebulizer O2 is still on. No need for additional meds at this point.
Back in pain at about a 2? Still localized to the left thigh?
Yes, same location-no new sensation or swelling.
No new medication changes or symptoms mentioned. Allergies and GCS remain stable.
We'll slowly move you up to the stretcher using a draw sheet, keeping you in a semi-Fowler's position for breathing.
You'll remain in direct harnesses for support and security during transport.
We will directly transfer you by stretcher into the ER at [COMPANY_NAME]. No unnecessary movement.
Arrival at the hospital confirmed everything as advised over the phone. ER nurse Jillian has been briefed.
Patient is a 66-year-old female named Jane Doe, born June 13, 1953, at 102A.
Jane currently uses an IV in the left arm, received 10 mg Dexamethasone, and is on O2 via mask.
She has a history of chronic anxiety, GERD, and hyperlipidemia, but denies chest pain or cardiac issues.
GCS is 15 with an initial tachypnea and tachycardia, and overall improving vitals on transport.
RN Jillian has met us at the ER and is ready to receive care and documentation.
All notes were filled in real time-avoids missing or duplicate entries.
